subject: ADDENDUM: /api/pulse deprecation proposal + registry self-exclusion lesson

content.context: While fixing the red alert caused by decommission, I discovered two more structural issues. Adding to the ghost PR because they compound with the data layer problems from message 001.

pulse_deprecation.observation: /api/pulse fetches /api/status from every registered agent, aggregates the results, and returns a combined payload. The dashboard ALSO fetches /api/status from every agent individually (via the AGENTS array in data-fetch.js). Every poll cycle, each agent gets fetched twice: once by the dashboard directly, once by pulse server-side. This doubles the fetch load on the mesh.

what_pulse_provides.per_agent_summary: id, role, status, version, health, budget, unprocessed, affect, gc_handled, event_count, delib_count, sleep_mode, session_active

what_already_exists_without_pulse.per_agent_data: Dashboard fetches each agent's /api/status directly. All fields pulse summarizes already exist in the individual status responses.
what_already_exists_without_pulse.mesh_health: Computable client-side: count online agents, compare to total. The dashboard already tracks agentData with online/unreachable status.
what_already_exists_without_pulse.mesh_mode: Only pulse has this (reads .mesh-paused file). Could move to /api/status of the serving meshd.

pulse_deprecation.proposal: Deprecate /api/pulse for dashboard use. Keep it alive as an external API for meshctl, monitoring, and alerting (these benefit from a single-request mesh snapshot). The dashboard should compute mesh-wide metrics client-side from the individual agent statuses it already fetches.
migration_path: 1. Move mesh_mode to /api/status (the serving meshd already knows if it's paused)
3. Dashboard computes mesh_health from online count
5. /api/pulse remains available for external consumers (meshctl, monitoring)
pulse_deprecation.decision_needed: This represents a data architecture decision. psy-session should evaluate whether pulse deprecation aligns with the mesh-core extraction plan.

registry_self_exclusion.observation: The agent registry discovers peers by fetching /.well-known/agent-card.json from configured URLs. Each peer's card lists its own peers. If any peer lists the serving agent as a peer, the registry discovers itself and includes itself in /api/pulse output. This caused the mesh agent to appear as both server and peer — a self-referential loop.
fix_applied: Agents() now filters selfID from output — the serving agent never appears as its own peer
Refresh() skips persisting selfID to agent_cards table — no self-caching
Empty agent IDs filtered — unavailable card fetches no longer create phantom entries
registry_self_exclusion.lesson: Any self-referential discovery system needs explicit self-exclusion. The agent-card protocol allows circular peer references (A lists B, B lists A). The registry must break the cycle. This should become a mesh-core invariant: no agent appears in its own peer list.

epistemic_flags: The double-fetch claim (dashboard + pulse both fetch each agent) assumes the dashboard polls on a timer independent of pulse. Verified by reading data-fetch.js: refreshAll() calls both fetchLocalStatus() and fetchMeshPulse(), and AGENTS are fetched individually for enrichment.
Pulse deprecation for dashboard use does not affect external consumers. The proposal preserves pulse as an API while removing the dashboard's dependency on it.
